TextBlock.Text: SOVIETS SEIZE U.S. NAVY SHIP—AMERICAN FORCES STRIKE BACK
U.S. Submarine Sinks Soviet Warships as Special Forces Retake Captured Vessel

StackPanel.TextBlock:
WASHINGTON (AP) - In a dramatic turn of events, U.S. military forces have struck back against Soviet aggression in the Norwegian Sea, sinking multiple Soviet warships and successfully reclaiming the seized U.S. Navy vessel, USNS Cape Girardeau.
The crisis began early yesterday when Soviet warships forcibly boarded the Cape Girardeau, an unarmed Military Sealift Command vessel conducting routine operations in international waters. The ship and its crew of over 80 were taken by force, with Moscow falsely claiming the vessel was engaged in espionage.
But within hours, the United States launched a decisive response. According to Pentagon officials, two Norwegian submarines tracked the Soviet task force as it attempted to retreat toward Soviet waters. In a lightning fast engagement, the submarines engaged the task group, with at least one of the vessels reported sunk and the remaining ones heavily damaged.
As the Soviet fleet reeled from the sudden assault, U.S. special operations forces launched a daring mission to retake the Cape Girardeau. Under the cover of darkness, Navy SEALs and Delta Force operatives, deployed from helicopters and stormed the captured vessel. After a brief but intense firefight with Soviet naval troops aboard the ship, American forces secured the bridge, neutralized enemy resistance, and freed the hostages.
Pentagon sources report that fifteen Americans were killed in the action, and several U.S. special forces operators sustained injuries in the operation. Soviet casualties remain unconfirmed, but intelligence suggests that several enemy personnel were either killed or captured during the engagement.
In a televised address to the nation, President Reagan hailed the operation as a “resounding victory for freedom and justice,” stating, “The United States will never allow Soviet aggression to go unanswered. We have defended our sailors, upheld international law, and made it clear that acts of piracy will not be tolerated.”
Moscow has reacted with outrage, accusing the U.S. of “reckless escalation” and threatening severe consequences. The Kremlin’s propaganda outlets claim that the Soviet warships were attacked without provocation and have vowed retaliation. NATO forces across Europe and the North Atlantic are now on their highest state of alert, with additional U.S. carrier battle groups reportedly moving into position.
With the Cold War now hotter than ever, the world holds its breath, uncertain of what will come next. Will cooler heads prevail, or has the conflict entered a point of no return?
